facts.text: Kaser was founded in 1990 by the Viznitz Hasidic dynasty to create denser housing for their growing community.
The village sits entirely within the hamlet of Monsey, making it a unique enclave surrounded by a larger community.
Kaser’s name comes from the Yiddish word for “crown,” reflecting its founders’ aspirations for a prominent, self‑governed village.
Early development focused on rapid housing construction, with land bought through communal funds instead of individual mortgages.
Zoning disputes with Ramapo officials over the 1990s led to Kaser’s incorporation, giving the community local control over its growth.
Kaser’s residents average nearly five people per household, a reflection of the high fertility rates typical in Hasidic communities.
